projectsDetailed.name: ICCA 2028
projectsDetailed.oneLiner: Led a 2-person team to ship a composable congress site — 80% of pages live in two weeks
projectsDetailed.context:
ICCA 2028 is the biennial congress of the International Council for Commercial Arbitration — one of the most prestigious gatherings in international law, held in a different world city every two years. The 2028 edition is set for San Francisco.

The organization needed a website that served several high-stakes goals simultaneously: introduce the congress and the city to a global professional audience, convert visitors into pre-registrations, drive attendance when registration opened, and attract corporate sponsors by clearly communicating sponsorship value and making it easy for them to reach the organizing team — all under a compressed delivery timeline.
projectsDetailed.role: Lead developer on a two-person team. Responsible for defining the technical architecture, establishing the full toolchain and deployment pipeline, coordinating the Git workflow across both environments, and mentoring my colleague throughout — all while delivering the build under a tight deadline.
projectsDetailed.approach:
Defined a composable architecture from the start: a custom WordPress theme with ACF as the component layer, built on DRY principles so that every element was built once and reused across the site rather than duplicated per page. The design was delivered in Figma; from those files we extracted design tokens — color, typography, and spacing values — that fed directly into the PostCSS pipeline as CSS custom properties, keeping the implementation aligned with the design system throughout. PHP templates handled server-side rendering for each content area; jQuery and direct DOM manipulation covered interactive front-end behaviors.

Set up PostCSS for the CSS pipeline and Bitbucket Pipelines for automated deployment to two separate environments — development and staging. The staging environment was deliberately designed to protect the content editor: their work in staging was never overwritten by development commits, letting content and code advance in parallel without conflict.

Third-party integrations included Osano for cookie consent and privacy compliance, DigiOh for the lead capture forms powering pre-registration and sponsor contact, and Google Maps to showcase the San Francisco congress venue and surroundings.
projectsDetailed.challenges:
The first phase — 80% of the site — had to launch two weeks after development began. That left no room to revisit architecture: the composable ACF system had to be right from the start, and pages had to be built fast without accumulating technical debt.

Leading a team under that timeline meant actively mentoring my colleague on the toolchain — PostCSS, Bitbucket Pipelines, Git workflows — while simultaneously building. Keeping velocity high while maintaining a coherent, shared codebase required clear conventions and continuous communication.

techStack.name: WordPress
techStack.note: CMS foundation with a custom theme and page templates for each distinct section of the congress site.

techStack.name: PHP
techStack.note: Server-side template rendering following the WordPress template hierarchy across every page type.

techStack.name: ACF
techStack.note: Composable component layer — reusable, configurable field groups assembled across pages without duplication, enforcing DRY throughout the codebase.

techStack.name: PostCSS
techStack.note: CSS pipeline for compilation and file watching; Figma design tokens — color, typography, and spacing — compiled into CSS custom properties to keep the implementation aligned with the design system.

techStack.name: jQuery / JS
techStack.note: Interactive behaviors and direct DOM manipulation across the front end.

techStack.name: Bitbucket Pipelines
techStack.note: CI/CD for automated deployment to development and staging environments, enabling parallel content and code workflows without conflicts.

techStack.name: Osano
techStack.note: Cookie consent and privacy compliance platform integrated across all pages.

techStack.name: DigiOh
techStack.note: Lead capture forms for pre-registration and sponsor contact, directly serving the site's primary conversion goals.

techStack.name: Google Maps
techStack.note: Embedded maps showcasing the San Francisco congress venue and surrounding area.

projectsDetailed.outcomes: Phase one — 80% of the site — delivered on schedule, two weeks after kickoff. The dual-environment setup let the content editor work in staging uninterrupted while development continued in parallel. My colleague reached full productivity with the toolchain before we hit the deadline.

projectsDetailed.name: PartySlate
projectsDetailed.oneLiner: Zero-downtime blog integration with a custom Gutenberg block library
projectsDetailed.context: PartySlate had an existing blog and main site. They wanted a modernized editorial experience and a fresh WordPress installation integrated into their existing infrastructure — without any downtime during the transition.
projectsDetailed.role: WordPress developer responsible for the full integration: new WordPress setup, custom Gutenberg block library, ACF template architecture, and GraphQL-powered widget implementation.
projectsDetailed.approach:
Set up a new WordPress installation and integrated it with both their main site and their legacy WordPress blog using shared authentication. Built a custom Gutenberg block library that gave the editorial team a structured, flexible content workflow tailored to their publishing patterns.

ACF field groups standardized template output across post types. GraphQL endpoints fed interactive widgets throughout the site with real-time data from their existing API.
projectsDetailed.challenges: Running both the new and legacy WordPress installations in parallel without downtime required careful routing and shared state management during the cutover window. Mapping the GraphQL schema cleanly to the existing data model took several iterations to get right.

techStack.name: WordPress
techStack.note: Fresh installation integrated with the legacy WP blog and main site, using shared authentication across all three.

techStack.name: Gutenberg Blocks
techStack.note: Custom block library tailored to the editorial team's content workflows and publishing patterns.

techStack.name: ACF
techStack.note: Template logic and structured field groups for consistent, predictable output across post types.

techStack.name: GraphQL
techStack.note: Real-time data layer powering interactive widgets throughout the site, pulling from their existing API.

techStack.name: PHP
techStack.note: Server-side rendering for blocks, templates, and REST endpoints.

techStack.name: JavaScript
techStack.note: Block interactivity, GraphQL query execution, and frontend widget logic.

projectsDetailed.outcomes: Zero downtime on launch day. The editorial team adopted the new Gutenberg blocks immediately, reducing publishing friction. The GraphQL widgets delivered live data without additional infrastructure.

projectsDetailed.name: PalTech
projectsDetailed.oneLiner: Full PSD-to-WordPress build, pixel-accurate across every breakpoint
projectsDetailed.context: PalTech needed a complete website rebuild from finalized PSD designs. Every page had a distinct layout — the project demanded accurate translation of each design into a maintainable, scalable codebase.
projectsDetailed.role: Lead developer and sole engineer — responsible for everything from architecture and tooling through launch, plus ongoing post-launch support and maintenance.
projectsDetailed.approach:
Worked from the finalized PSD designs to define the architecture before writing a line of code: identified the custom post types the site required, mapped out the templates each needed, and catalogued the components and sections that could be shared across layouts. Built a custom WordPress theme using an approximation of atomic design — WP template parts served as the composition layer, with ACF Pro field groups driving the content behind each reusable component. This kept the codebase DRY across a project with many distinct templates without duplicating markup or styles.

The asset pipeline ran on Gulp: compiling Sass, autoprefixing, minifying CSS and JS, and watching for changes during development. jQuery and animate.css handled UI interactions and scroll-triggered entrance animations.
projectsDetailed.challenges: Pages shared components but not always structure — the challenge was designing a component library expressive enough to cover every layout variation while keeping the codebase DRY. Each template had to be composable from shared parts without duplicating markup or styles. Making the backend genuinely usable for a non-technical client required careful ACF field design and labeling throughout.

techStack.name: WordPress
techStack.note: CMS with custom post types, template hierarchy, and WP template parts as the composition layer for each page.

techStack.name: ACF Pro
techStack.note: Flexible field groups powering every reusable component — structured content the client can manage without touching code.

techStack.name: PHP
techStack.note: Custom template files and template parts following the WordPress template hierarchy, composed per page from shared components.

techStack.name: Sass
techStack.note: Organized, maintainable CSS across a large variety of layouts, compiled and processed by Gulp.

techStack.name: Gulp
techStack.note: Asset pipeline — compiling Sass, autoprefixing, minifying CSS and JS, and watching files during development.

techStack.name: jQuery
techStack.note: Frontend UI interactions, DOM manipulation, and triggering animate.css effects on scroll.

techStack.name: animate.css
techStack.note: CSS animation library for scroll-triggered entrance effects without heavy JavaScript overhead.

projectsDetailed.outcomes: Launched on time. The composable ACF system made post-launch maintenance low-overhead, and the client kept me on for ongoing support and maintenance indefinitely.

projectsDetailed.name: All Weather
projectsDetailed.oneLiner: Complex multi-post-type site with dynamic Masonry layouts
projectsDetailed.context: A complex WordPress site with multiple custom post types and heavy dynamic layout requirements. Delivered as a coordinated team effort under real-world time constraints.
projectsDetailed.role: Lead front-end developer, with backend contributions alongside the development team throughout the project.
projectsDetailed.approach:
Built custom ACF templates for each distinct post type, ensuring structured, predictable output regardless of content length or type. Masonry.js was integrated with jQuery to handle dynamic grid layouts that adapt to content quantity and card dimensions — the grid reflows correctly on pagination and dynamic content loads.

Worked closely with backend developers throughout to align data structure and display logic without blocking either side of the stack.
projectsDetailed.challenges: Getting Masonry.js to re-initialize reliably after WordPress pagination and dynamic content changes required careful event sequencing with jQuery. Delivering clean front-end output under a tight timeline while coordinating with backend developers who were building the data structures in parallel.

techStack.name: WordPress
techStack.note: CMS with multiple custom post types for varied editorial content categories.

techStack.name: ACF
techStack.note: Custom field groups per post type, driving template-level output for each content category.

techStack.name: PHP
techStack.note: Server-side rendering across custom templates tied to the WordPress template hierarchy.

techStack.name: jQuery
techStack.note: UI interactions and triggering Masonry re-layouts when content changes or pagination fires.

techStack.name: Masonry.js
techStack.note: Dynamic, content-aware grid layout for mixed card sizes — accommodates variable content lengths cleanly.

techStack.name: CSS
techStack.note: Custom styles layered on top of the theme foundation for each content type and layout.

projectsDetailed.outcomes: Delivered on schedule under real project constraints. Dynamic Masonry layouts performed reliably across all content types and viewport sizes. The coordinated team delivery kept both frontend and backend in sync throughout.

projectsDetailed.name: WP FlashNotes
projectsDetailed.oneLiner: A WordPress plugin for structured learning and self-study
projectsDetailed.context: A personal project targeting submission to the official WordPress plugin repository. WP FlashNotes introduces structured learning tools — note blocks and flashcard blocks — directly into the Gutenberg editor.
projectsDetailed.role: Sole developer — block API architecture, server-side rendering, and the JavaScript-driven quiz interface.
projectsDetailed.approach: Built on the WordPress block API using React for the editor interface. Notes and flashcards can be grouped into study sets using the block's InnerBlocks API. Server-side rendering ensures content is accessible without JavaScript. The quiz interface is a JavaScript layer on top of the rendered output.
projectsDetailed.challenges: Designing the study set grouping architecture using block nesting in a way that felt natural in the editor. Meeting plugin repository standards for code quality, security, and internationalization.

techStack.name: WordPress
techStack.note: Plugin foundation, block registration, and REST API endpoints for quiz state.

techStack.name: Gutenberg Blocks
techStack.note: Custom block types using the WordPress block API — note blocks, flashcard blocks, and study set containers.

techStack.name: React
techStack.note: Block editor interface and inspector controls, following Gutenberg's React-based extension model.

techStack.name: PHP
techStack.note: Server-side rendering and plugin bootstrap — ensuring content is accessible without client-side JavaScript.

techStack.name: JavaScript
techStack.note: Quiz interface layer on top of server-rendered flashcard output.

projectsDetailed.outcomes: In active development. Core block types functional and usable. Working toward the code quality and documentation standards required by the official WordPress plugin repository.

projectsDetailed.name: KadenceWP

projectsDetailed.context: KadenceWP is one of the most widely-used theme and block plugin suites on WordPress.org. As part of the StellarWP team, I contributed to Kadence Blocks, Kadence Blocks Pro, and the Kadence theme across multiple release cycles.
projectsDetailed.role: WordPress developer on the StellarWP product team — bug fixes, feature development, and code review across the Kadence ecosystem.
projectsDetailed.approach: Worked within an established codebase serving a large user base, which required careful change management and thorough testing before each release. Shipped new Gutenberg block features using the WordPress block API and React, and resolved PHP-side issues in template rendering and the Options API.
projectsDetailed.challenges: Shipping changes to a plugin used by hundreds of thousands of sites — every change required understanding downstream effects and writing regression-safe code. Working within a large, pre-existing codebase without introducing breaking changes.

projectsDetailed.name: ICCA 2028
projectsDetailed.oneLiner: Lideré un equipo de 2 personas para lanzar un sitio de congreso composable — 80% en línea en dos semanas
projectsDetailed.context:
ICCA 2028 es el congreso bienal del Consejo Internacional de Arbitraje Comercial (International Council for Commercial Arbitration) — uno de los encuentros más prestigiosos del derecho internacional, celebrado en una ciudad diferente del mundo cada dos años. La edición 2028 tendrá lugar en San Francisco.

La organización necesitaba un sitio web que cumpliera varios objetivos de alto impacto simultáneamente: presentar el congreso y la ciudad a una audiencia profesional global, convertir visitantes en pre-registros, impulsar la asistencia cuando se abriera el registro, y atraer patrocinadores corporativos comunicando con claridad el valor de patrocinar el evento y facilitando el contacto con el equipo organizador — todo bajo un plazo de entrega ajustado.
projectsDetailed.role: Desarrollador líder en un equipo de dos personas. Responsable de definir la arquitectura técnica, establecer el toolchain completo y el pipeline de despliegue, coordinar el flujo de trabajo con Git en ambos entornos, y mentorear a mi colega durante todo el proceso — mientras entregábamos el proyecto bajo un plazo ajustado.
projectsDetailed.approach:
Definí una arquitectura composable desde el inicio: un theme personalizado de WordPress con ACF como capa de componentes, construida bajo principios DRY para que cada elemento se construyera una sola vez y se reutilizara en todo el sitio. El diseño fue entregado en Figma; a partir de esos archivos extrajimos design tokens — valores de color, tipografía y espaciado — que alimentaron directamente el pipeline de PostCSS como custom properties de CSS, manteniendo la implementación alineada con el sistema de diseño durante todo el proyecto. Las plantillas PHP manejaron el renderizado del lado del servidor para cada área de contenido; jQuery y manipulación directa del DOM cubrieron los comportamientos interactivos del frontend.

Configuré PostCSS para el pipeline de CSS y Bitbucket Pipelines para el despliegue automatizado a dos entornos separados: desarrollo y staging. El entorno de staging fue diseñado deliberadamente para proteger al editor de contenido: su trabajo en staging nunca era sobreescrito por los commits del equipo de desarrollo, lo que permitió que contenido y código avanzaran en paralelo sin conflictos.

Las integraciones de terceros incluyeron Osano para el consentimiento de cookies y cumplimiento de privacidad, DigiOh para los formularios de captación de leads que impulsan el pre-registro y el contacto de patrocinadores, y Google Maps para mostrar el venue del congreso en San Francisco y sus alrededores.
projectsDetailed.challenges:
La primera fase — el 80% del sitio — debía lanzarse dos semanas después de comenzar el desarrollo. Eso no dejó margen para revisar la arquitectura: el sistema composable de ACF tenía que ser correcto desde el principio, y las páginas tenían que construirse rápidamente sin acumular deuda técnica.

Liderar el equipo bajo ese plazo implicó mentorear activamente a mi colega en el toolchain — PostCSS, Bitbucket Pipelines, flujos de Git — mientras construíamos simultáneamente. Mantener la velocidad sin perder la coherencia del código compartido requirió convenciones claras y comunicación constante.

projectsDetailed.outcomes: La fase uno — el 80% del sitio — se entregó puntualmente, dos semanas después del inicio. La configuración de dos entornos permitió al editor de contenido trabajar en staging sin interrupciones mientras el desarrollo continuaba en paralelo. Mi colega alcanzó plena productividad con el toolchain antes de llegar al plazo.

projectsDetailed.name: PartySlate
projectsDetailed.oneLiner: Integración de blog sin interrupciones con biblioteca de bloques Gutenberg
projectsDetailed.context: PartySlate tenía un blog y sitio principal existentes. Querían una experiencia editorial modernizada y una nueva instalación de WordPress integrada en su infraestructura existente, sin interrupciones durante la transición.
projectsDetailed.role: Desarrollador WordPress responsable de la integración completa: nueva configuración de WordPress, biblioteca de bloques Gutenberg personalizados, arquitectura de plantillas ACF e implementación de widgets con GraphQL.
projectsDetailed.approach:
Configuré una nueva instalación de WordPress y la integré con su sitio principal y blog WordPress legado usando autenticación compartida. Construí una biblioteca personalizada de bloques Gutenberg adaptada a los flujos de trabajo editorial del equipo de contenido.

Los grupos de campos ACF estandarizaron la salida de plantillas. Los endpoints GraphQL alimentan widgets interactivos en todo el sitio con datos en tiempo real.
projectsDetailed.challenges: Ejecutar ambas instalaciones de WordPress en paralelo sin interrupciones requirió gestión cuidadosa del enrutamiento durante el cambio. Mapear el esquema GraphQL limpiamente al modelo de datos existente requirió varias iteraciones.

projectsDetailed.outcomes: Sin interrupciones el día del lanzamiento. El equipo editorial adoptó los nuevos bloques de Gutenberg de inmediato. Los widgets GraphQL entregaron datos en tiempo real sin infraestructura adicional.

projectsDetailed.name: PalTech
projectsDetailed.oneLiner: Construcción completa de PSD a WordPress, pixel-perfecta en cada breakpoint
projectsDetailed.context: PalTech necesitaba una reconstrucción completa de su sitio web desde diseños PSD finalizados. Cada página tenía un layout distinto — el proyecto exigía traducción precisa de cada diseño en un código mantenible y escalable.
projectsDetailed.role: Desarrollador líder y único ingeniero — responsable de todo desde la arquitectura y el tooling hasta el lanzamiento, más soporte y mantenimiento post-lanzamiento continuo.
projectsDetailed.approach:
Partí de los diseños PSD finalizados para definir la arquitectura antes de escribir una línea de código: identifiqué los custom post types que el sitio requería, mapeé las plantillas que cada uno necesitaba y catalogué los componentes y secciones que podían compartirse entre layouts. Construí un theme personalizado de WordPress usando una aproximación al diseño atómico — las template parts de WP funcionaron como la capa de composición, con los grupos de campos ACF Pro manejando el contenido detrás de cada componente reutilizable. Esto mantuvo el código DRY a lo largo de un proyecto con muchas plantillas distintas sin duplicar markup ni estilos.

El pipeline de assets corrió sobre Gulp: compilando Sass, autoprefixing, minificando CSS y JS, y haciendo watch de archivos durante el desarrollo. jQuery y animate.css manejaron las interacciones UI y animaciones de entrada activadas por scroll.
projectsDetailed.challenges: Las páginas compartían componentes pero no siempre la estructura — el reto fue diseñar una biblioteca de componentes lo suficientemente expresiva para cubrir cada variación de layout manteniendo el código DRY. Cada plantilla debía poder componerse desde partes compartidas sin duplicar markup ni estilos. Hacer el backend genuinamente usable para un cliente no técnico requirió un diseño cuidadoso de los campos ACF a lo largo de todo el proyecto.

projectsDetailed.name: WP FlashNotes

projectsDetailed.context: KadenceWP es uno de los suites de theme y plugins más utilizados en WordPress.org. Como parte del equipo StellarWP, contribuí a Kadence Blocks, Kadence Blocks Pro y el theme Kadence en múltiples ciclos de lanzamiento.
projectsDetailed.role: Desarrollador WordPress en el equipo de producto StellarWP — corrección de bugs, desarrollo de funcionalidades y revisión de código en todo el ecosistema Kadence.
projectsDetailed.approach: Trabajé dentro de una base de código establecida que sirve a una gran base de usuarios, lo que requirió gestión cuidadosa de cambios y pruebas exhaustivas antes de cada release. Publiqué nuevas funcionalidades de bloques Gutenberg usando la API de bloques de WordPress y React, y resolví problemas PHP en el renderizado de plantillas y la Options API.
projectsDetailed.challenges: Publicar cambios en un plugin usado por cientos de miles de sitios — cada cambio requería entender los efectos downstream y escribir código seguro ante regresiones. Trabajar dentro de un código preexistente grande sin introducir cambios disruptivos.

techStack.name: WordPress
techStack.note: Plataforma principal — trabajé en hooks, filtros, Options API y renderizado de plantillas.

techStack.name: Bloques Gutenberg
techStack.note: Extensiones de la API de bloques y nuevas funcionalidades publicadas en Kadence Blocks y Kadence Blocks Pro.

techStack.name: React
techStack.note: Componentes del editor de bloques, controles del panel inspector y UI de barra lateral.

techStack.name: PHP
techStack.note: Correcciones de backend en renderizado de plantillas, Options API y salida de bloques del lado del servidor.

techStack.name: JavaScript
techStack.note: Comportamiento de bloques en el frontend, extensiones del editor e interactividad para nuevas funcionalidades.

projectsDetailed.outcomes: Múltiples funcionalidades y correcciones de bugs publicadas en ciclos de lanzamiento. Cambios entregados a un suite de plugins en producción usado por cientos de miles de sitios WordPress.
